description:
Kaggle environment wrapper for OpenSpiel games.
For game implementation details see:
https://github.com/google-deepmind/open_spiel/tree/master/open_spiel/games
name: open_spiel_go

action.description: Action object MUST contain a field `submission`, and MAY contain arbitrary additional information.

actTimeout.description: Maximum runtime (seconds) to obtain an action from an agent.

episodeSteps.description: Maximum number of steps in the episode.

includeLegalActions.description: If true, include legalActions and legalActionStrings in observations. Defaults to false since these can be derived from serializedGameAndState.

initialActions.description: Actions applied to initial state before play begins to set up starting position.

loadPresetHands.description: Repeated poker only. Load preset hand chance actions from preset_hands.jsonl.

openSpielGameName.description: The short_name of the OpenSpiel game to load.

openSpielGameParameters.description: Game parameters for Open Spiel game.

openSpielGameString.description: The full game string including parameters.

presetHands.description: Repeated poker only. List of per-hand chance action sequences to use instead of random chance.

runTimeout.description: Maximum runtime (seconds) of an episode (not necessarily DONE).

seed.description: Integer currently only used for selecting starting position.

useImage.description: If true, indicates the observation is intended to be rendered as an image. Note that currently the agent harness is responsible for the actual rendering; no image is passed in the observation.

useOpenings.description: Whether to start from a position in an opening book.

currentPlayer.description: ID of player whose turn it is.

legalActionStrings.description: List of OpenSpiel legal action strings. Only included if includeLegalActions is true.

legalActions.description: List of OpenSpiel legal action integers. Only included if includeLegalActions is true.

openSpielGameName.description: Short name of the OpenSpiel game.

playerId.description: ID of the agent receiving this observation.

serializedGameAndState.description: Enables reconstructing the Game and State objects.

remainingOverageTime.description: Total remaining banked time (seconds) that can be used in excess of per-step actTimeouts -- agent is disqualified with TIMEOUT status when this drops below 0.

action.thoughts:
Based on the current board state, we are playing on a 13x13 board. Black's first move was K10, which occupies the 4-4 point in the upper right corner. 

For my first move as White, it is standard opening theory to claim another empty corner. Occupying the opposing corner at D4 (the 4-4 point in the lower left) provides a balanced and flexible start, setting up for rapid development and influence across the board. 

```json
{
  "move": "d4"
}
```

action.thoughts:
In Go, the 13x13 board size emphasizes efficiency and solid territory. The game has just begun with move 1 being Black at k10 (a star point in the upper right) and move 2 being White at d4 (a star point in the lower left). 

Standard opening theory for a 13x13 board suggests that Black should continue by occupying another of the corner star points. These points (d4, k4, d10, k10) are strategically important as they offer a balance between territory in the corner and influence toward the sides and center. 

Since k10 and d4 are already occupied, Black's most logical choices are the remaining star points: k4 or d10. Both moves are symmetrically equivalent in this setup. Playing at k4 establishes a presence on the right side of the board and mirrors White's influence in the lower left, maintaining a balanced distribution of power. 

Given the high komi of 7.5, Black must play efficiently to maintain the initiative. Taking another corner star point is a solid and standard approach that secures territory while keeping the game open for further development.

```json
{
  "move": "k4"
}
```

action.thoughts:
The game is being played on a 13x13 board (as shown by the board coordinates in the JSON, despite the mention of 9x9). 
In the opening phase of a game on a 13x13 board, it is standard practice to occupy the corners first, usually on the 4-4 or 3-4 points. 

Looking at the board state:
- Black has played K10 (the 4-4 point in the top-right).
- White has played D4 (the 4-4 point in the bottom-left).
- Black just played K4 (the 4-4 point in the bottom-right).

The top-left corner is the last remaining unoccupied corner. Playing at D10 (the 4-4 point in the top-left) is the most natural and balanced move. It mirrors the other stones, staking a claim in the final corner and setting up a solid framework for the middlegame. 

```json
{
  "move": "d10"
}
```

action.thoughts:
The current game state indicates that the game is in its final stages, with most of the board being occupied by established groups. Black has a significant amount of territory in the center and right side, while White holds territory on the left, top, and far-right. The large Black group (encompassing columns J and K and extending to parts of columns D-G) is well-connected and has sufficient space to form eyes in the empty region around E6-F9. White's groups are also mostly secure, with ample liberties or connections to the edges. 

After evaluating the board, there are no immediate threats to Black's groups, and most remaining moves are either filling dame (neutral points) or points within a player's own established territory. White's last move was a2, which is an endgame move on the bottom-left edge. Black could respond by filling a dame or an endgame point, but PASS is appropriate if the board is considered settled. Given the instructions to play the strongest move and the near-completion of the game, passing is a standard action when no more significant gains can be made.

```json
{
  "move": "PASS"
}
```

action.thoughts:
The previous move played by Black was a PASS, indicating that they believe the board is settled and there are no further profitable moves to be made. 

Looking at the current board state, the boundaries are securely defined with no remaining dame (neutral points) to fill, no vulnerable groups to defend, and no viable weaknesses in the opponent's territory to exploit. Since any further play within our own territory would just cost us points (under Japanese scoring rules) and any play in the opponent's territory would be captured, passing is the correct decision to end the game and proceed to scoring.

```json
{
  "move": "PASS"
}
```
